---
title: "First Responder Presumptive Laws"
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-banner}
::: {.hero-kicker}
ALERRT Center · Texas State University
:::

# When a First Responder<br>Gets Sick, Who Pays?

::: {.hero-lead}
Cancer. Heart disease. PTSD. First responders face these at higher rates than the general population — but getting workers' compensation often means proving your job caused it. **Presumptive laws shift that burden**, treating these illnesses as work-related by default. Coverage varies widely across all 50 states.
:::

[Explore the Dashboard →](map.qmd){.hero-cta}
:::

::: {.stat-bar}
::: {.stat-item}
[50]{.stat-number}
[states covered]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-item}
[6]{.stat-number}
[condition types]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-item}
[5]{.stat-number}
[occupations tracked]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-item}
[400+]{.stat-number}
[law entries]{.stat-label}
:::
:::

## What is a presumptive law?

Under standard workers' compensation rules, a sick first responder must demonstrate that their specific job directly caused their illness — a nearly impossible bar for diseases like cancer or heart disease that develop over years. A **presumptive law** eliminates that requirement: the illness is *presumed* to be work-related, and the burden falls on the employer to prove otherwise.

Most presumptions are *rebuttable* — the employer can present contradicting evidence. A small number are *irrebuttable*, meaning the link is conclusive regardless of other evidence. Coverage also depends on which conditions are listed in the statute, which occupations qualify, how many years of service are required, and whether there's a post-retirement window.

::: {.section-intro}
Keeping track of where these protections exist — and what they actually cover — is harder than it should be. This dashboard is an attempt to fix that.
:::

## How to use this app

::: {.how-to-grid}
::: {.how-to-card}
[01]{.card-num}
**Pick a mode.** Use *Occupation* to ask which conditions are covered for a specific responder type. Use *Condition* to ask which states cover a specific illness — and for whom.
:::
::: {.how-to-card}
[02]{.card-num}
**Read the chart.** The intersection diagram shows how many states cover each combination of responder groups. Hover a bar to see which states; click to lock the selection.
:::
::: {.how-to-card}
[03]{.card-num}
**Click the map.** Selecting a state opens a detailed table of all laws on record — statute citations, service requirements, post-employment coverage, and notes.
:::
::: {.how-to-card}
[04]{.card-num}
**Verify before acting.** Laws change. Always confirm current status with a qualified attorney or your state's workers' compensation board.
:::
:::

## About this project

This dashboard was developed by the [ALERRT Center](https://alerrt.org) at Texas State University. ALERRT conducts applied research on first responder training, health, and wellness.

The underlying data come from the [IAFF Presumptive Health database](https://www.iaff.org/presumptive-health/), maintained by the International Association of Fire Fighters. We've structured and mapped that information to make it easier to search and compare — but the IAFF pages remain the authoritative source. See the [About](about.qmd) page for full methodology and citation.

<span class="proto-badge">Prototype</span> This is an early-stage research tool. If you notice an error or a law that has changed, the [About](about.qmd) page explains how to interpret the data.
